summary: Joel Spolsky shares two brief observations: a warning against believing media narratives that economic downturns are permanent, and detailed advice on writing effective cover letters. He argues that Fortune magazine's claim that job-finding has permanently changed is the same cyclical nonsense as the 'New Economy' hype of the late 1990s. On cover letters, he explains that generic, template-driven letters signal desperation and laziness, while personalized letters that show genuine interest in the specific company signal that the candidate is a top-tier hire with options.
key_points: Economic cycles are inevitable and centuries-old; media claims that 'this time is different' are always wrong, whether during booms or busts
Generic cover letters following standard career-services advice make candidates indistinguishable and signal mass-application desperation
The best candidates demonstrate they have choices and are applying because of genuine interest in the specific company, not out of desperation
Personalizing a cover letter to show knowledge of the company and dropping hints that you're selectively applying puts you in the top 1% of applicants
95% of cover letters Fog Creek receives show no sign of being customized for the job
money_quotes: The sure sign that things are about to change is when the conventional wisdom becomes, 'things will never change.'
Don't take any of the standard career-services-office advice for writing cover letters, or your cover letter will look exactly like everyone else's cover letter.
95% of the cover letters I receive do not include anything about Fog Creek and show no sign that they have been customized in any way for the job in question.
This sends a signal that you are simply spamming your resume to hundreds of jobs, which, in turn, sends a signal that you are both desperate and not willing to work very hard.

The very best candidates have come to realize that they have a choice of where to work, and when they apply for a job, they are applying because there's something intriguing about that particular job.
If you make your cover letter interesting, make it personal, and drop hints that you have choices in the world, you will sound more like one of the top 1% candidates.
key_insight: The best job candidates signal their quality by showing genuine, specific interest in a company rather than broadcasting desperation through generic applications.
